steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

The doubled middle letter shows doing something with extra effort or completeness. Here it changes the meaning from physical movement to spiritual praise, showing complete dedication to declaring Allah's perfection.
This is the base word الله (Allah) with the shadda on the ل to show emphasis
Added لِ at the beginning to show this is for or belongs to Allah. The first ا is removed when لِ is added.
Added the kasra (ِ) at the end to show this connects to what comes after it in the sentence. This word tells us who is being glorified in سَبَّحَ (glorified).
This is a special word that refers to non-living things. Here it means 'whatever exists' in the heavens.
Basic preposition showing location or position
The ى at the end makes this word specifically about being inside something. In this ayah, it's talking about what exists inside the heavens.
Adding fatha marks creates the basic past tense verb pattern
Root سمو (meaning height/elevation) changed to سماوات by adding ا and plural ending ات to make 'heavens'
Added ٱل to make it specific
Added kasra at end to show this word is connected to what comes after it
This is a relative pronoun that refers to all things in the earth mentioned later in the ayah
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with the previous similar phrase about what is in the heavens (مَا فِى ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ)
Basic preposition showing location or position inside something. Here it shows what exists within the heavens.
Added kasra (ِ) under ف to show how to pronounce it correctly
Changed the final ي to ى which is just a writing style difference - it means the same thing
Connected the root letters together with vowel marks to make it a basic noun
Adding ٱلْ makes it refer to the specific earth rather than any earth. The ٱ (alif with waṣla) is used instead of ا because it comes at the start of the word.
The ِ ending shows this word follows the word فِى (in/within) that comes before it in the ayah
This is a basic pronoun meaning 'he' (subject of العَزِيزُ الحَكِيمُ - the Mighty, the Wise)
Adding 'وَ' at the beginning connects this word to what came before it, joining it to the previous statement about everything in the heavens and earth glorifying Allah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Added ي between second and third root letters to show this is a strong quality

steps.m: The Mighty One (Divine attribute of Allah)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and ُ at the end to show this is the subject. This is one of Allah's names describing His perfect might and power.
Added ي between second and third root letters to show someone or something having the quality of wisdom
Added ال to make it definite and ُ to show it's describing Allah. This is one of Allah's names showing His perfect wisdom.

steps.m: to point to or indicate something
which (used to point to something specific)
steps.n: The root letters combine and the middle ي doubles to make a word that asks 'which one?'
Added يا with a long sound (ٰ) and a small alif (ٓ) to show we're calling out to someone
Added vowel marks (َ and ُ) to show this is addressing someone directly
Added ها to make it address a group instead of one person
This is the basic form of the word that means 'who' or 'which' when talking about one person or thing
Added ين at the end to make it refer to many people instead of just one
Added ال at the beginning to make it more specific, like saying 'those who' instead of just 'who'
Added final vowel marks and changed spelling slightly: 1) ا became ٱ at start, 2) added ّ on ل, 3) added َ at end. This word connects to ءَامَنُوا۟ (believed) to tell us who believed.
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
The long alif at start changes it from 'believe' to 'make someone believe'

steps.m: they believed (referring to ٱلَّذِينَ - those who)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show many people did this action. This word describes 'those who' (ٱلَّذِينَ) from earlier in the verse.
This combines two parts: لِ (for/to) and مَا (what) to create a question word
The final alif is dropped in common usage, making it shorter and easier to say. This word is asking about the action تَقُولُونَ (you say) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make the past tense form. The middle و changes to ا because it's a weak letter.
Changes from 'he said' to 'you say' by adding تَ at the start
Adds masculine plural suffix to indicate 'you all'
This is a relative pronoun that can mean 'what' or 'that which'
Added fatha mark to show clear pronunciation of first letter
This is the basic negative particle that means 'not'. In this ayah, it negates the verb تَفْعَلُونَ (you do), creating the meaning 'you do not do'
Added fatha (َ) on ل to show how it's pronounced
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense base form
Changed to present tense with ت prefix for 'you' and adjusted vowels
Added ون to show multiple people are being addressed
The ُ (damma) in the middle shows this is a natural quality
Changes from verb 'to hate' into the noun 'hatred'

steps.m: hatred (as something greatly disliked by Allah)
steps.n: Adding ًا shows this word is indefinite and is being acted upon by the verb كَبُرَ (it became great/grievous). The full meaning in context is that this type of hatred became something very serious/grievous with Allah.

steps.m: to be present, to be near
steps.n: This word is used to show location or possession, like saying something is 'at' or 'with' someone
Added vowels to make it a preposition. This word connects to اللَّهِ (Allah) to show something is with/near Allah.
Added ال to specify this is THE one true God, and the hamza was dropped due to frequent usage

Added kasra at the end because this word follows عِندَ (with/at). The ل has a shadda to emphasize it, and the initial alif has a waṣla mark because it connects with the previous word in pronunciation.

steps.m: that (introduces what is said in تَقُولُوا)
steps.n: This is a basic particle that connects two parts of a sentence. Here it introduces what is being criticized - saying things without doing them.

steps.m: that (introduces what is said in تَقُولُوا)
steps.n: Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense form
Changed from past tense (he said) to present tense (saying) by changing the middle vowels from 'aa' to 'uu'
Added تَ at the beginning to show 'you' are doing the action
Added وا at the end to show many people are doing the action together
This is a relative pronoun or interrogative particle
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation. In this ayah, مَا connects 'what you say' with 'what you do not do', showing the mismatch between words and actions.
This is a negative particle that negates the verb تَفْعَلُونَ (you do) that comes after it. Together they mean 'you do not do'.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding تَ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action
This is an emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows. In this ayah, it emphasizes that Allah truly/definitely loves those who fight in His cause.
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ل, transforming this into the unique name for The One True God

steps.m: Allah (as the object of يُحِبُّ - loves)
steps.n: Changed initial alif to wasl form (ٱ), added shadda on ل, and added fatha at end to show Allah is the object being loved in this verse
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense form
The doubled middle letter shows intensity of the action
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. This word describes what Allah does in the phrase 'Indeed, Allah loves'
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things
Added ال (the) at the start to make it mean 'the one who' instead of just 'who'
Added ين at the end to make it plural. This word describes the people who are fighting (يُقٰتِلُونَ) in Allah's cause.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create past tense verb
Adding alif shows two people fighting each other
Adding يُ at start and changing vowels shows ongoing action
Adding ون shows multiple people doing the action
Basic preposition showing location or position. In this ayah, it connects to سَبِيلِهِۦ (His path) to show 'in His path'
Adding ي and vowels creates the pattern for 'path'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it (His)

steps.m: His path/way (in فِى سَبِيلِهِۦ - in His path)
steps.n: Added هِۦ at the end to show the path belongs to Allah. This word is part of the phrase 'in His path' (فِى سَبِيلِهِۦ)

steps.m: to line up or arrange in order

steps.n: The doubled letter shows this is done with force or repeatedly

steps.m: in a row (describes how they fight in يُقٰتِلُونَ)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this describes how the fighting is done - they fight in an organized row
Added fatha to show it's a particle meaning 'like' or 'as'
Added أَنَّ with shadda to combine with كَ to mean 'as if'
Added هُم at the end to show this applies to 'them'
Changed from verb 'to build' into noun 'building' by adding damma and sukun marks
Added ان to make it mean a complete building/structure, with ٌ showing it's the main subject

steps.m: to pack or join things tightly together

steps.n: The doubled ص shows extra strength in the packing/joining action

steps.m: something that has been packed/joined tightly
steps.n: Changed to show something that received the action of being packed/joined tightly

steps.m: something that has been packed/joined tightly (indefinite)
steps.n: Added tanween dammah to make the word indefinite

steps.m: something that has been firmly packed/joined (like a solid structure)
steps.n: Added shadda on the م to emphasize how solid and firm the joining is

steps.m: when, at the time (introduces the statement of Moses)
steps.n: This is a particle that refers to a past time. It introduces the story of when Moses spoke to his people.

steps.m: and when (introduces the statement of Moses)
steps.n: The و connects this story to what came before it. It shows this is another example being given after previous examples.
This is the basic meaning showing the action of speaking or saying something
Added fathas to make it past tense and show who did the action
The middle letter و changes to ا because it comes between two fathas. This is a common change that makes the word easier to say.

steps.m: Moses (the prophet who spoke to قَوْمِهِ/his people)
steps.n: A proper noun borrowed from Hebrew, not derived from an Arabic root

steps.m: Moses (the prophet who spoke to قَوْمِهِ/his people)
steps.n: Added damma on first letter and fatha on second letter to show proper pronunciation

steps.m: Moses (the prophet who spoke to قَوْمِهِ/his people)
steps.n: Added alif khanjariya (small alif) above the final letter to show proper pronunciation

steps.m: to stand/rise (basic meaning that evolved to mean group that stands together)
steps.n: The root meaning of standing/rising is transformed into a collective noun for people who stand together
Added vowels to the root letters to make a noun meaning 'a group of people who stand together'
Added لِ at beginning to show direction/purpose
Added kasra (i sound) at the end because something comes after it to show ownership - like saying 'people of'

steps.m: to/for his people (referring to Moses's people)
steps.n: Added هِۦ to show these are specifically Moses's people
Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter to create a noun meaning 'people'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is the object of a preposition
Adding يٰ (yaa) at the start turns this into 'O my people!' - it's like saying 'Hey my people!' to get their attention
Combined the preposition لِ (for/to) with ما (what) to form a question word

steps.m: why? (asking about تُؤْذُونَنِى - you harm me)
steps.n: The ا is dropped at the end, which is common in Arabic questions. This word is asking about why the people are harming Moses.
Connected the root letters together
Changed from base form to 'he' present tense with yu- prefix
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يُ with تُ
Changed to plural 'you all' form
Added 'me' as the object receiving the action
This is a particle that adds emphasis or shows something is complete. Here it emphasizes that they 'certainly know' (تَّعْلَمُونَ).
The و connects this phrase to what came before it, adding the meaning of 'and'
This is the basic concept of knowing or having knowledge
Changed from basic root 'know' to past tense 'he knew' by adding the vowel marks
Changed to present tense and second person (you) by adding تَ at start and changing vowels
Added ونَ to show multiple people are being addressed
Added تَ to show doing the action for oneself - learning/acquiring knowledge
This is the base emphatic particle that shows certainty or introduces a statement
Added the first person pronoun ي at the end with a kasra before it to show this is about 'I/me'. This connects to the next words which explain that 'I am the messenger of Allah'
This pattern فَعُول is used for nouns that describe someone who does something intensively or as their main role
Adding vowel marks to complete the فَعُول pattern which indicates someone who performs an action intensively
Adding the u-sound at the end shows this word is doing the action
Added shadda to emphasize this is the one and only God
Added kasra at the end to show this connects to the word رَسُولُ (messenger) before it. The alif at the start changed to a special form (ٱ) because it's connected to the word before it.
Basic preposition indicating direction or destination
The ى changes to ي with sukun when connecting to pronouns
Added كُمْ which means 'you all' - used when speaking to multiple people
This is a connecting word that shows when something happened. In this ayah, it connects to the word زَاغُوٓا۟ (they deviated), showing when they deviated.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened as a result of something before it, like saying 'and then' or 'so then' in English

steps.m: to turn away from the right path

steps.n: Added vowels and changed middle letter ي to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)
Added وا to show multiple people did the action
These marks help show it's a plural verb

steps.m: to turn away from the right path

steps.n: Added vowels to make it past tense and show who did the action

steps.m: he caused (someone) to turn away (subject of اللَّهُ)
steps.n: Adding أَ at the beginning makes it mean 'causing someone else to do the action' instead of doing it yourself. Here Allah is the one causing the turning away.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding vowel marks
This is the unique name for the one true God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes the ل, and the ُ shows this is the subject of the sentence.

steps.m: to turn over, to change direction

steps.n: Forms the basic noun 'heart' - named this way because it constantly turns blood over in the body
Changes to broken plural form meaning 'hearts'
Adds هُمْ to show possession (their), with َ connecting it to the word
Connected the root letters to form the basic word for deity
Added a double L sound (shadda) to change from 'any deity' to specifically mean Allah, the one true God

steps.m: Allah (as the doer of أَزَاغَ - made to deviate)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show Allah is the one doing the action (making their hearts deviate) in this ayah

steps.m: and Allah (as the doer of أَزَاغَ - made to deviate)
steps.n: Added 'and' (و) at the beginning to connect this part to what came before
A negative word that makes the verb يَهْدِى (guides) into 'does not guide'. In this ayah, it's saying that Allah does not guide the disobedient people.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added يَ at the beginning to show present tense, changed middle vowel to sukun, and last vowel to kasra to match present tense pattern
Added ى at the end to show it's a present tense verb that ends with a weak letter
Added fatha and sukun to form the noun 'people' from the concept of standing/establishing together

steps.m: the people (object of لَا يَهْدِى - does not guide)
steps.n: Added ال to make it definite and fatha at the end to show it's the object receiving the action of 'not guiding' in the phrase 'Allah does not guide the people'
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb

steps.m: one who breaks away from obedience (acted on by يَهْدِى)
steps.n: Changes verb to describe someone who does this action. This word is being acted on by the verb يَهْدِى (guides) in this ayah.

steps.m: those who break away from obedience
steps.n: Makes the word plural to show we're talking about multiple people

steps.m: the disobedient ones (acted on by يَهْدِى)
steps.n: Adds 'the' to specify these particular disobedient ones, and adds the final vowel to show this word is being acted upon by يَهْدِى (guides)
This is a compound particle made of 'wa' (and) + 'idh' (when)
Added fatha marks to show past tense and who did the action
Changed و to ا because it's a hollow verb (has weak middle letter)

steps.m: Jesus (son of Mary, mentioned in قَالَ)
steps.n: This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew/Aramaic 'Yeshua/Isa'. In this ayah, he is speaking to the Children of Israel as a messenger of Allah.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'son' by adding alif at the beginning. The meaning connects to the root because a son is someone 'built' or 'established' from their parents.
Added the sukun and damma marks to show this is the subject of قَالَ (he said) in this ayah. Also changed the initial alif to hamzat al-wasl (ٱ) since it's only used to help pronunciation.
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew/Aramaic. In this ayah, it's part of the phrase 'Jesus son of Mary' (عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ).

steps.m: Mary (mother of عِيسَى, possessed by ٱبْنُ)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows that Mary is being possessed by the word ٱبْنُ (son), forming the phrase 'son of Mary'
Adding vowel marks to create basic past tense verb form
Added يٰ (yaa) at the start to show someone is being called out to. Changed from 'he built' to 'O sons!' since this is a different word entirely - بَنِي here means 'sons' not 'built'.

steps.m: Israel (the prophet Jacob, peace be upon him)
steps.n: This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew meaning 'servant/wrestler of God' or 'one who struggles with God'. It refers to the prophet Jacob (يعقوب), peace be upon him.
This is a basic emphasis word that makes statements stronger, like adding 'truly' or 'definitely' in English
Added ى at the end to show 'I' am speaking. In this ayah, Prophet Isa (Jesus) is speaking to the Children of Israel.
The و makes it mean someone who does this action as their main role
Added ُ at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence. This word is the subject of قَالَ (he said) in this ayah.
Added shadda to emphasize this is the one and only God
Added kasra at the end to show this belongs to something else. Here it shows Allah is the one who the messenger (رَسُولُ) belongs to.
This is a basic preposition showing direction or destination

steps.m: to you all (addressing the Children of Israel)
steps.n: Added كُم to show this is addressing multiple people. In this ayah, it's specifically addressing the Children of Israel when Jesus is speaking to them.
Doubled the middle letter to show doing something with extra strength or effort
Added مُ at start and changed middle vowel to show who does the action. This describes Jesus (عِيسَى) in this verse.
Added ًا at the end and a shadda on م to show this describes Jesus as someone who confirms what came before him
This is an emphasis particle that adds certainty to what follows
The لَ combines with another ل that means 'for', creating a shadda. The kasra shows it means 'for'
Adding ما makes it mean 'what'. In this ayah, it connects to مُّصَدِّقًا (verifying), showing what Isa (AS) was verifying
The root carries the basic idea of separation or distinction between things
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the preposition form. This word shows location in relation to يَدَىَّ (my hands/what is before me)
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by another word in the sentence
The root letters form the word for 'hand' when you add the vowel mark fatha (َ)
Adding ى at the end makes it mean 'my two hands'
Added shadda and fatha to show these are my hands. This word is the object of بَيْنَ (between) in this ayah.
This is a basic preposition meaning 'from' that connects to the word التَّوْرَىٰةِ (the Torah)
The َ (fatha) is added because the next word starts with ال. This helps connect the words smoothly when reading.
Changed alif to alif maqsura with small alif above (ىٰ) which is a common spelling variation
Added ٱلْ at the beginning to make it specific, and ِ at the end because it follows the word مِنَ (from/of) in this ayah

steps.m: to bring lots of good news
steps.n: Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger
Adding مُ at start and changing middle vowel to ِ shows who does the action
Adding ًا (tanween) at the end makes this a direct object - something receiving the action

steps.m: and a bearer of good news (object of إِنِّى رَسُولُ)
steps.n: Adding وَ connects this word to what came before it. This word describes Jesus's role as mentioned in 'I am a messenger'
The و makes it mean 'someone who does this action a lot'

steps.m: by/with a messenger (describing how أَحْمَدُ will come)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show this messenger is the means by which something will happen. This connects to the verb يَأْتِى (will come) in the ayah, showing how Ahmad will come.
Connected the root letters together

steps.m: he comes (future action in this ayah)
steps.n: Added يَ at beginning to show present/future tense, with sukun and kasra to complete the pattern. In this ayah, it refers to a future coming.
Added sukoon on last letter to show it's in jussive mood, which often happens after certain particles
This is a helper word that shows something starts from a particular point or comes from somewhere. Here it connects with the word بَعْدِى (after me) to show 'from after me'.
Added a small mark (called a 'sukun') after the ن to show where to stop when reading. This doesn't change the meaning.
Changes from root letters to basic noun meaning 'after'
Added kasra at the end to prepare for the possessive pronoun

steps.m: after me (referring to Prophet Jesus speaking about Prophet Muhammad)
steps.n: Added ى at the end to show this means 'my/me'. In this ayah, Prophet Jesus is talking about Prophet Muhammad who would come after him.

steps.m: to name, to mark, to be elevated

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to name' into the noun 'name'
Changes spelling of initial alif to help pronunciation
Added هُ meaning 'his' and the elongation mark ۥٓ. This word is the subject in the phrase 'his name is Ahmad'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb

steps.m: most praiseworthy (name of the Prophet ﷺ that will come after عِيسَى)
steps.n: The root word 'he praised' becomes a special form meaning 'most praiseworthy' by adding أ at the start. This became the name 'Ahmad' that Jesus said would be the name of the Prophet ﷺ who comes after him.

steps.m: when (connects to جَآءَهُم - came to them)
steps.n: This is a time particle that shows when something happened. In this ayah, it connects the time when 'he came to them' (جَآءَهُم).

steps.m: then when (connects to جَآءَهُم - came to them)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens as a result of or following something else. Here it shows what happened after Jesus spoke.
Basic past tense verb pattern with fatha marks

steps.m: he came to them (referring to the Children of Israel mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added هُم at the end to show who he came to. This refers back to the Children of Israel (بَنِى إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ) mentioned earlier in the verse.
Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to make it into a noun meaning clear evidence
Added ات to make it plural feminine, showing multiple clear proofs
Added ال to make it definite (specific proofs)

steps.m: with/by the clear proofs (object of جَآءَهُم - came to them)
steps.n: Added بِ at beginning to show 'with/by' and changed final vowel to kasra because it follows a preposition. The word is the object that 'came to them' in this verse.
This is the basic concept of speaking or saying something
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas - this is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning
The ُ (damma) at the end shows this word is in the nominative case, like the subject of a sentence

steps.m: they said (subject of هٰذَا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ)
steps.n: Added وا to show multiple people did this action. In this ayah, this refers to the people who rejected the clear signs, and they are the ones saying 'This is clear magic'
Basic demonstrative element meaning 'here' or 'this'
Added ذا to specify this is for pointing to masculine things that are close by
Added alif with bar (ٰ) and fatha marks to complete the word. This word is pointing to سِحْرٌ (magic) in this ayah.
Added kasra (ِ) under first letter and sukun (ْ) on middle letter to change from action to noun

steps.m: magic (describes what they called the clear signs)
steps.n: This word describes what the disbelievers called the clear signs (بِٱلْبَيِّنٰتِ) that were shown to them. They said 'This (هٰذَا) is magic.'
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past-tense verb
Added مُ at start and doubled the ي to show someone who does the action
Changed from 'one who makes clear' to 'clear/obvious' by adding shadda on م and tanween at end. Describes the magic (سِحْرٌ) in this ayah.
This is a relative pronoun used to ask about or refer to people
The و connects this word to what came before. This word is asking 'who' in relation to being أَظْلَمُ (more wrong/unjust) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb

steps.m: more/most unjust (describing someone who invents lies against Allah)
steps.n: Changes to comparative/superlative form by adding أَ at start, putting sukun on first root letter, and adding ُ at the end. This word describes someone mentioned in مِمَّنِ (than who) that follows it.
Basic preposition meaning 'from'
Combined with مَن (who). The ن from مِنْ combines with م from مَن, shown by the shadda (ّ)
Added ِ at the end to connect it to the next word in the sentence. This word is asking about who does the action افْتَرَىٰ (invents/fabricates)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added letters (ا and ت) change meaning from 'cut' to 'made up/invented' - like cutting up truth to create lies
This is an orthographic change to clarify pronunciation of the final ى
Basic preposition showing that the lie (ٱلْكَذِبَ) is being made against or attributed to Allah. In this context, it shows someone falsely attributing something to Allah.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters
Added kasra (small diagonal line) under the last letter to show this is Allah's guidance. Connected to the word 'guides' in this ayah.
Adding vowels turns the root into a complete past tense verb
Added 'the' to make it specific. This word is what is being invented/fabricated (افْتَرَىٰ) against Allah in this verse.
Third person masculine singular pronoun. This pronoun is the subject of يُدْعَىٰٓ (is called/invited) in this ayah.
Adding conjunction و
Adding the basic vowel pattern that makes this a past tense verb
Added يُ at start to make it present tense, and changed inside vowels to show someone is being called (passive) instead of doing the calling
Changed the ending to ى which is common for passive verbs
Added small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's a long vowel sound
Basic preposition showing direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects to الْإِسْلٰمِ (Islam) to show someone is being called towards Islam.

steps.m: to submit peacefully, to be at peace

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he submitted' (verb) to 'submission' (noun) by adding إِ at start and ا in middle

steps.m: the Islam (religion of submission to Allah)
steps.n: Added 'the' to make it definite and ِ at the end because it follows إِلَى (to) in the ayah
This is a unique proper name that doesn't come from a root. It's the personal name of God in Islam.
Added shadda on ل to show it's pronounced strongly

steps.m: God (as the subject of لَا يَهْدِى/does not guide)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence

steps.m: and God (as the subject of لَا يَهْدِى/does not guide)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this part to what came before it
Basic negative particle that negates the verb يَهْدِى (guides) in this ayah, making it mean 'does not guide'
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Added fathas to connect the letters. The ي changes to ى at the end because it's a weak letter.

steps.m: he guides (subject of يُدْعَىٰٓ - he is called)
steps.n: Added يَ at start to show present tense. The middle letter gets a sukun (ْ) and the letter after it gets a kasra (ِ) in present tense. This word describes what the person does who is being called to Islam.

steps.m: to stand together, to rise up as a group

steps.n: The root meaning of standing together becomes a word for a group of people who stand/rise together. This word is receiving the action of guidance (يَهْدِى) in this ayah.

steps.m: the people (who Allah does not guide)
steps.n: Added ال at the beginning to mean 'the' and َ at the end to show this word receives the action of 'not guiding' from Allah
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters

This pattern shows someone who does the action - like adding '-er' in English (teach → teacher). The word is in accusative case because it is the object (the thing receiving the action) of the verb يهدي (guides).
The ين suffix makes this a masculine plural noun
Added ٱل (the) at the beginning and َ at the end. The ظ gets doubled (shadda) because it's a sun letter after ٱل
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense form
Added يُ at start to show present tense, and changed the middle letter to make it easier to say
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This word is connected to لِيُطْفِـُٔوا (in order to extinguish) showing their intention.
Basic meaning of extinguishing something
Changes from root letters to past tense verb meaning 'he put out'
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing middle vowel to ئِ
Added plural suffix وا to indicate multiple doers

steps.m: so that they may put out (the نُورَ ٱللَّهِ - light of Allah)
steps.n: The لِ shows this is their purpose or goal - they want to put out Allah's light. This connects to يُرِيدُونَ (they want) earlier in the ayah.
Basic meaning of light or illumination
Changed from the root meaning 'shine' into the noun 'light' by adding the vowel ُ (u) and joining the letters together

steps.m: light (object of لِيُطْفِـُٔوا - they want to extinguish)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb 'extinguish' in the phrase 'they want to extinguish'
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters
Added kasra at the end to show possession. This word describes whose light (نُورَ) they are trying to extinguish.
Basic meaning related to mouth or speaking
The root letters change to make the basic word for 'mouth'
This is a broken plural form for body parts - it changes the internal structure of the word to show multiple
Added بِ at the beginning to show these mouths are being used as tools or instruments

steps.m: with their mouths (trying to extinguish نُورَ ٱللَّهِ)
steps.n: Added هِمْ to show these are their mouths. This word is part of how they are trying to extinguish Allah's light (نُورَ ٱللَّهِ)
This is a unique word that started as the general word for deity (إله) but became the proper name for the One God. The doubled ل makes it special.
Added fatha on the doubled ل to show how it's pronounced

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of مُتِمُّ - the One who completes)
steps.n: The ُ at the end shows that Allah is the one doing the action (completing His light) in this verse

steps.m: And Allah (as the subject of مُتِمُّ - the One who completes)
steps.n: Added 'and' (وَ) at the start to connect to the previous part

steps.m: to complete or make something perfect
steps.n: Basic meaning of completion or perfection
Adding vowel marks makes it a proper past tense verb meaning 'he completed'

steps.m: one who completes (subject of نُورِهِۦ - His light)
steps.n: Added مُ at start and changed vowels to show who does the action. This word describes Allah completing His light despite the disbelievers' attempts to extinguish it.
Basic meaning of light or illumination
Changed from 'giving light' (action) to 'light' itself (noun) by adding ُ and و
Added ِ to show this word belongs to or is connected to مُتِمُّ (the one completing/perfecting)
Added هِ to show this light belongs to Allah (His light)

steps.m: even if (connecting to يُرِيدُونَ - they want)
steps.n: This combines two particles: و (and) + لو (if). When used together, they create the meaning 'even if' or 'although'. Here it connects to the previous statement about them wanting to extinguish Allah's light.
Basic meaning of hatred or dislike

steps.m: he hated/disliked (subject of ولو - even though)
steps.n: Added vowels to show this is a complete past tense verb. This word comes after ولو (even though) in the ayah, showing contrast.
Added vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed verb to show who does the action. This describes someone who actively rejects truth.
Changed to plural to show many people who reject truth

steps.m: the rejecters (of truth) (who hate نُورَ ٱللَّهِ)
steps.n: Added 'the' to point to specific group. In this verse, they are the ones who hate Allah's light (نُورَ ٱللَّهِ).
This is a simple word that means 'he'. In this ayah, it refers to Allah who is the one that sent His messenger. It's like saying 'He is the one who...' in English.
This is the basic form of the word that points to someone or something
Adding ٱلّ makes it more specific, like saying 'the one who' instead of just 'who'
The long vowel mark (ٓ) helps with pronunciation when connecting to the next word
Adding basic vowel pattern for Form I past tense verb
Adding أَ at the beginning makes this a causative verb - showing that someone made the action happen. In this ayah, it refers to Allah making His messenger (رَسُولَهُۥ) deliver the message.
The root ر-س-ل (to send) becomes رَسُول (messenger) by adding vowels, then the final َ (fatha) shows someone is doing something to the messenger
Adding هُ shows the messenger belongs to Him (Allah). The ۥ is just a pronunciation helper.

steps.m: to guide or direct to the right path

steps.n: The root letters were changed into a noun meaning 'guidance' by adding the vowels u and a (هُدَى). The ي at the end becomes ى when written at the end of a word.
Added بِ ('with/by') and ال ('the') at the beginning. The ٰ at the end shows this is a special form of ى.

steps.m: to follow a path or way

steps.n: Root letters د-ي-ن became the noun 'religion' by adding kasra (ِ) under د and a long ي sound
Added kasra at the end to show connection to the word الْحَقِّ (the truth) that comes after it
Added وَ at the beginning to mean 'and', connecting this word to what came before it
Added fatha and doubled the middle letter to create a noun meaning 'truth' or 'what is right'
Added 'the' at the beginning and kasra at the end to show it belongs to 'religion' in the phrase 'religion of truth'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Adding alif at start makes the action affect something else - like changing 'appear' to 'make appear'
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Changed ending vowel to fatha to show this is dependent on something before it
Added هُ at the end to show what is being made to appear

steps.m: so that He makes it prevail (over دِينِ)
steps.n: Added لِ at start to show purpose - 'in order to' or 'so that'. The word relates to دِينِ (religion) in this ayah.
This is a preposition that shows something is above, superior to, or has authority over something else. Here it shows that something will prevail over or dominate all religions.
Added kasra and sukun to make it into a noun meaning a system of beliefs
Added ٱل to make it specific ('the') and added shadda to strengthen the د. Added kasra at the end because this word follows 'على' in the ayah.
The doubled ل (shadda) makes the meaning stronger, showing it means absolutely everything with no exceptions
Adding the kasra (ِ) shows this word is connected to الدِّينِ (the religion) that owns or possesses it
Adding هِ shows this refers to 'it' - referring back to الدِّينِ (the religion)
This is a conditional particle that introduces a hypothetical situation. Added vowel marks to show proper pronunciation.
Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect this condition with the previous statement. Together وَلَوْ means 'and even if/though', showing that what follows doesn't change the main point.
Adding vowel marks to show this is a past tense feeling verb

steps.m: to join or associate things together

steps.n: Adding vowels makes this a past tense verb showing someone joined things together

steps.m: someone who joins others with Allah in worship
steps.n: Added مُ at the start and changed vowels to show this describes someone who does this action

steps.m: multiple people who join others with Allah in worship
steps.n: Added ون at the end to show we're talking about multiple people

steps.m: the polytheists (those who worship others alongside Allah)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ at the start to point to specific polytheists and َ at the end because it's the subject of كَرِهَ (disliked)
This is a vocative expression combining multiple parts
Root letters combined with fatha and shadda (doubling mark) to make a question word meaning 'which/who'
Added يٰٓ at the beginning to make it a call for attention
Added damma at the end to show it's in the nominative case
Adding ها makes it specifically address a group of people
This is a relative pronoun used to describe a group of people
Basic vowels (fat-ha) added to make it 'he believed' in past tense
Adding long alif at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'
Added plural masculine ending وا to show multiple people did the action. This word describes 'those who' (ٱلَّذِينَ) being addressed in the ayah.
This is an interrogative particle used to ask yes/no questions

steps.m: to show the way or guide

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding أ at start makes it mean 'to cause/make someone do the action'

steps.m: I guide you all (addressing the believers)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show who is being guided - 'you all' referring to the believers being addressed in this verse
This is a preposition that shows something is on top of or higher than something else. Here it connects 'guide you' to 'trade', meaning 'guide you to trade'.
Added an alif khanjariyah (ىٰ) at the end, which is just a writing style that doesn't change the meaning
Changed the word from an action to a concept by adding specific vowels and an alif
Added ة at the end to show we're talking about one specific instance of trade

steps.m: a trade/business (that will save you from عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this is indefinite (like 'a' or 'an' in English). The word is connected to عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (painful punishment) in the ayah, showing what this trade will save you from.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense verb
Added shadda to show intensive saving/rescuing, and kasra to prepare for next changes
Added ت prefix to make it present tense (she/you form)

steps.m: it saves/rescues you all (from عَذَابٍ - punishment)
steps.n: Added كُم at the end to show who is being saved. This word connects to عَذَابٍ (punishment) in the ayah to show what they're being saved from.
Basic preposition indicating source or origin
The 'n' sound is doubled (gets a shadda) because it comes right after a word ending with a vowel sound
Added sukun at the end to show the word stops here. This preposition connects to the word عَذَابٍ (punishment) to show what we're being saved from.
Changed the verb into a noun showing the result of the action
Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end because it comes after the word مِنْ (from) in the ayah
Added vowels and ي to create an intensive adjective form that shows something causes a lot of pain. This word describes the punishment (عَذَابٍ) mentioned in the ayah.
Added the tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this word follows and describes عَذَابٍ (punishment) in the ayah

steps.m: to trust, believe in, feel secure with
to cause to believe/trust, to make secure
steps.n: Adding alif at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'
Changed from past tense 'believed' to present tense 'you believe' by adding تُ at start. The ؤ comes from the original hamza. Shows you (singular) believing in Allah and His messenger.
Changes from 'you' (one person) to 'you all' (group) by adding ون at the end
Connected the root letters to form the basic word for deity
Added shadda on ل to create the unique name Allah, which is only used for the one true God
Added fatha on the shadda to help with pronunciation
Changed to show Allah is being acted upon (notice the small i sound at the end)

steps.m: in/by/with Allah (object of تُؤْمِنُونَ - you believe)
steps.n: Added the preposition بِ at the beginning to show belief in Allah. This word is connected to تُؤْمِنُونَ (you believe) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from a verb (sending) to a noun (person who sends/delivers messages)
This word is in the genitive case because it is possessed by Allah (الله). The word رسول means messenger, and the ه at the end means 'His', referring to Allah.
The conjunction و (and) is added to connect this word with the previous phrase

steps.m: to strive or struggle with maximum effort

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Added alif after first letter to show increased intensity of action
تُ shows this is happening now (present tense) and is done by 'you' or 'she'
Added plural marker to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: and you (all) strive hard (connected to تُؤْمِنُونَ)
steps.n: Added connecting particle to link with previous word تُؤْمِنُونَ (you believe)

steps.m: in (connects سَبِيلِ with what comes before)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows location or position. Here it connects the word سَبِيلِ (path) to the previous words, showing what you believe and struggle in.

steps.m: in (connects سَبِيلِ with what comes before)
steps.n: Added kasra (ِ) under ف to show how it's pronounced

steps.m: in (connects سَبِيلِ with what comes before)
steps.n: Changed the ي to ى because it comes at the end of the word. This is just a writing style change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Adding vowel marks creates the basic past tense verb meaning 'he made a way'
Adding ي changes this from a verb (to make a path) into a noun (a path)
Added kasra at end because it follows فِى (in/on) and comes before اللَّهِ (Allah)
Added shadda to emphasize the uniqueness and greatness of Allah
Changed the first letter to hamzat al-wasl (ٱ) and added kasra at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the word بِ (in/with) in the ayah
Adding the fatha vowel mark helps show this is about having/possessing wealth
Changed to broken plural form to show multiple properties/wealth
Changed the word to prepare it for connection with the following pronoun
Added plural possessive pronoun 'your'
Added preposition 'with/by' at the beginning
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters into a basic noun form
This is a broken plural form used for important body parts and personal attributes
Added suffix to show possession by multiple people
Added 'and' to connect this word to 'your wealth' (أَمْوٰلِكُمْ) that came before it
The basic demonstrative pronoun with an alif written as a dagger alif (ٰ) to show distance
Added لِ to show more distance - changes 'this' to 'that'
Added كُمْ to show this is being said to multiple people
Changes from basic root to noun meaning 'good/goodness'
The double-damma (tanween) at the end shows this word is indefinite and is acting as the main subject - like saying 'a good thing' instead of just 'good'
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning
Added كُمْ at the end to show this is directed to multiple people
Basic conditional particle that connects two parts of a condition: 'if you believe...' with 'if you knew...'
Added kasra (ِ) under the إ to show correct pronunciation
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters
The weak letter و drops out, and we add تَ to show 'you' did this in the past
Changed تَ to تُمْ to show many people (you all) instead of just one person
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense and changed who is doing the action from 'he' to 'you'

steps.m: you all know/are knowing (referring to the believers being addressed in this ayah)
steps.n: Added ون to show that many people are being addressed, not just one person. This matches how the ayah is speaking to all the believers.
Added fathas to connect the root letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: he forgives/will forgive (connected to يُدْخِلْ - he will admit)
steps.n: Changed to present/future tense by: 1) adding يَ at start, 2) putting sukun on first root letter, 3) putting kasra on second root letter, and 4) putting sukun at end to show it's connected to another verb (يُدْخِلْ) in the ayah
Added fatha to show the vowel sound on ل
Added كُمْ which means 'you all'. This word is receiving the action of يَغْفِرْ (He will forgive) in this ayah - He will forgive for you all.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make the singular noun form
Changes singular 'sin' to broken plural form showing multiple sins

steps.m: your sins (object of يَغْفِرْ - he forgives)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show 'your' (plural) + فتحة (a) before it since it's being acted upon
Connected the root letters with fathas to make the basic past tense verb
Adding alif at start makes the action affect others - like making someone do something
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added كُمْ to show the action affects 'you all'

steps.m: and he makes you all enter (connected to يَغْفِرْ - he forgives)
steps.n: Added وَ to connect this action with the previous verb يَغْفِرْ (he forgives)
Adding vowel marks makes it a basic past tense verb
Added ة to make it mean 'a place that is covered/enclosed', which became the word for garden since gardens are enclosed spaces
Changed from one garden to many gardens by adding ات
Added ٍ (tanween kasra) to show these gardens are the object - the thing being entered into
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: it flows (referring to rivers under gardens)
steps.n: Added ت at start to show present tense, changed vowels to show ongoing action. This describes the rivers flowing under the gardens mentioned in the ayah.

steps.m: it flows (referring to rivers under gardens)
steps.n: Changed the final ي to ى which is just a writing style difference - meaning stays the same
This is a basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects the verb 'flows' (تَجْرِى) with 'beneath it' (تَحْتِهَا) to show where the flowing starts.
Added the kasra (ِ) vowel mark under the م to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'min'
Added vowel marks to show it follows the preposition مِن. The kasra at the end shows it's being affected by مِن.
Added ها to show this refers to the gardens (جَنَّاتٍ) mentioned earlier. The phrase مِن تَحْتِهَا means 'from beneath them (the gardens)'.
Added vowel marks to make the basic noun form for a single river
This changes the word to show many rivers using a special plural pattern
Added ال to show these are specific rivers being talked about
Added ُ to show this word is doing the action of flowing (تَجْرِى) in the verse

steps.m: to live in a place, settle down

steps.n: Added مَ at the start to show this is a place where something happens, like how 'kitchen' is a place for cooking
Added ا and changed vowels to make it plural, like changing 'house' to 'houses'
Added وَ at the start to connect it to the previous words about gardens (جَنَّاتٍ)

steps.m: and dwellings (connected to يُدْخِلْكُمْ - He will admit you)
steps.n: Added َ at the end to show this is part of what Allah will give, connected to the verb 'He will admit you'
Doubled the middle letter to show intensity
Added ة to make the word feminine
Added tanween to show this is describing the dwellings mentioned in the verse
Basic particle showing something is inside or within something else. In this ayah, it shows the rivers flow within/in the gardens.
Added ى at the end to complete the word. This is a common spelling for this word in the Quran. The word shows where the flowing (تَجْرِى) happens - in the gardens.

steps.m: to cover or hide (like how a garden's canopy covers what's beneath)

steps.n: Root letters compressed with shadda to make a word meaning 'to cover/hide'
Added ة to make it a feminine noun meaning 'a garden' (because gardens cover with shade)
Changed the ending to ات to show there are many gardens

steps.m: gardens (object of يُدْخِلْكُمْ - He will enter you into)
steps.n: Added ِ to show this word is being acted upon by the verb يُدْخِلْكُمْ (He will enter you into)
This word comes from the concept of permanently staying or dwelling somewhere. In the Quran, it refers to special gardens of Paradise where people will stay forever.

steps.m: permanent dwelling/residence (connected to جَنَّاتِ - gardens of)
steps.n: The tanween kasra (ٍ) shows this word is being described by or connected to another word (جَنَّاتِ meaning 'gardens of'). Together they mean 'gardens of permanent residence' - a name for a special level of Paradise.
Basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (dagger alif) above the ذ
Adding ل with kasra (ِ ) changes it from pointing to something near to something far

steps.m: that (referring to the rewards just mentioned)
steps.n: Adding ك with fatha (َ ) makes it specifically point to something mentioned before - in this case, the rewards of paradise and forgiveness that were just described
Changed from basic root letters to a noun meaning 'success' by adding vowels
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific
Added ُ at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence
Added vowels to make a past tense verb showing something becoming great
Changed the verb into an adjective describing something with greatness
Added 'Al' (ال) at the start to mean 'The' and makes it a title for Allah, with a u-sound ending (ُ) to show its importance in the sentence
Changed the root letters to make a simple word meaning 'other' by adding marks (damma and sukun)
Added ى at the end to make it feminine
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and'. This connects it to the word نَصْرٌ (victory) that comes after it in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
The middle letter is doubled (shadda) to show intensity of the love
Added تُ prefix for 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense
Added ونَ to show plural 'you all'

steps.m: you all love it/her (referring to another thing mentioned before)
steps.n: Added هَا to show what is being loved. This connects to أُخْرَىٰ (another thing) mentioned before it in the ayah.
Changed to noun form meaning the act or result of helping/supporting. This word is paired with فَتْحٌ (opening/conquest) in this ayah.
Added tanween (double damma) to make it an indefinite noun - meaning 'a victory' rather than 'the victory'
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or starts, like saying 'from' in English
Added a fatha at the end to connect it to the next word (اللَّهِ)
Added shadda for stronger pronunciation of the م
Changed from general word for deity to specific name for The One God by adding ال and merging letters
Added kasra at end to show possession, shadda on lam for emphasis, and changed alif to wasl-alif since it comes after another word. This word is possessed by نَصْرٌ (help/victory) in this ayah.
Added و at beginning to connect this word with نَصْرٌ (victory), showing they go together as 'victory and conquest'
Removes 'and' (wa) at start and adds two dots on top to make it 'a conquest'
Added ي between second and third root letters to make an adjective. This word describes the victory (نَصْرٌ) mentioned in the ayah.
Adding ٌ shows this is a general description, like saying 'a near victory' instead of 'the near victory'
Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to make it a command telling someone to give good news

steps.m: and give good news! (command to الْمُؤْمِنِينَ)
steps.n: Added 'and' at beginning and kasra at end to connect it with the following word الْمُؤْمِنِينَ (the believers)
Added مُ at the start and vowel marks to show this is someone who trusts/believes. This word is like changing 'trust' to 'one who trusts' in English.

steps.m: believers (object of بَشِّرِ - give good news to)
steps.n: Added ين at the end to show we're talking about many believers. This word receives the action of بَشِّرِ (give good news to) in this ayah.

steps.m: the believers (object of بَشِّرِ - give good news to)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ at the start to show we're talking about specific believers, not just any believers. The َ at the end shows this word receives the action of بَشِّرِ (give good news to).
This is used to call out to someone or get their attention
Two ي letters combine into one strong ي with shadda, turning it into a question word meaning 'which/who'
Vocative particle يا added to call attention, with long alif
Adds damma to show nominative case
Adds ها to make it address people directly
Starting with the basic demonstrative word and changing it to make it relative
Adding ال makes it specifically refer to someone or something
Adding ين makes it refer to multiple people
Adding the final vowel marks and changing the writing style of alif. This word is connected to ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed) in this ayah.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added an alif at the start to make it stronger - changes from basic 'trust' to deeper 'believe/have faith'
Added وا at the end to show many people did this
This is the basic concept of existing or being
Changed root letters into a command by adding damma (u) and sukun (stop mark) - like saying 'BE!'
The و and ن are added to make it plural, like saying 'you all be!' instead of 'you be!'
Added وا at the end to make this a command for a group
This is the basic meaning of helping or supporting
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and kasra under second letter to make it mean 'one who helps'
Changed from one helper to many helpers by adding أ at start and making the middle of the word أنصار
Added fatha at the end to show this word is the object of كُونُوا (be!)
Changed to the specific name for the One True God by adding ال and merging letters
Added kasra at the end to show possession, and added shadda on ل. This word is possessed by أَنصَارُ (helpers) in this ayah.
Added fatha to show how the letter is pronounced

steps.m: just as, like what (comparing this to what عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ did)
steps.n: Added ما with fatha to mean 'what', making the combined meaning 'just as' or 'like what'. This word is comparing the believers' actions to what Jesus son of Mary did with his disciples.
Added vowels to show this happened in the past and was done by 'he'. In this ayah, this word introduces what Jesus said to his disciples.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it comes between two fathas. This makes the word easier to say.

steps.m: Base form of the borrowed name Yeshua/Jesus
steps.n: This is the basic form of the name borrowed from Hebrew/Aramaic 'Yeshua', adapted to Arabic pronunciation.
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation: kasra (ِ) under ع and fatha (َ) over س
Added the ى (alif maqsurah) at the end to mark this as a proper noun in Arabic. This is a common ending for non-Arabic names adapted into Arabic.

steps.m: to build or establish family connections
steps.n: The root letters form the base of words related to building or establishing
Changed from the root meaning 'to build' into the basic word for 'son' by adding vowels (َ) and dropping the last letter ي
Added اِ at the start to make the common word for 'son' - like how we say ibn/ابن in names
Added ٱ at start so it can be used on its own
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew/Aramaic. In this ayah, it's mentioned as part of عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ (Jesus son of Mary).
The name Mary has a fatha mark at the end because she is being described as Jesus's mother

steps.m: to whiten, purify, select as special companion
steps.n: The root relates to the concept of purity and return
Changes from root letters to a noun meaning 'someone special/pure'

steps.m: disciple (one belonging to the specially chosen)
steps.n: Adds ي at the end to show 'someone who follows/belongs to' the chosen ones
Added ل (laam) to make 'to/for' stronger - like double emphasis
Added ين to make the word plural - now it means multiple disciples

steps.m: who (asking 'who are my helpers?' in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a question word used specifically for asking about people. Here, Jesus (عِيسَى) is asking the disciples (الْحَوَارِيِّين) who will be his helpers in calling to Allah.
The root relates to helping and supporting
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added alif after second root letter to create a noun meaning 'one who helps'
Added أ (hamza) at the beginning to make it plural - this is a common plural pattern in Arabic
Added kasra at the end to show this word is possessed by or connected to something
Added ى at the end to show these are 'my' helpers
This is a preposition showing direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects to the word اللَّهِ (Allah) to show 'to Allah'
Added shadda to emphasize this is the one and only deity worthy of worship

steps.m: of Allah (possessed by/connected to something else)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this word is possessed by or connected to something else in the sentence. The alif at the start changed to a special connecting form.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
The و changes to ا because it's a weak letter in the middle of the word

steps.m: to whiten, purify, or be loyal
steps.n: The root relates to purity and return
Changed root into a noun pattern that means 'someone who is pure/loyal'
Added a kasra vowel mark at the end to prepare for making it into an adjective
Added doubled ي to make it mean 'someone associated with being pure/loyal'
Added definite article ال

steps.m: the disciples (who said they would help Jesus)
steps.n: Added plural ending ون to show multiple disciples. In this ayah, these are specifically the disciples responding to Jesus asking who would be his helpers to Allah.

steps.m: we (the disciples saying 'we are Allah's helpers')
steps.n: This is a standalone pronoun meaning 'we'. In this ayah, it's being used by the disciples of Jesus to refer to themselves as a group when they declare their support.

steps.m: we (the disciples saying 'we are Allah's helpers')
steps.n: The ُ (damma) at the end shows this is the subject of the sentence, meaning it's doing the action
This is the basic meaning of the root
Adds alif after first letter and kasra before last letter to show who does the action, like changing 'help' to 'helper' in English
Changes the word to show many helpers by adding hamza at start and moving letters around, like changing 'helper' to 'helpers' in English
Adds damma (ُ) at the end to show these helpers are the main doers in the sentence. This word is what we're told to 'be' by the command كُونُوا in this ayah.
Added ال to specify this is THE one true deity, and the hamza was dropped because this word is used so frequently. The word became a proper noun specifically referring to Allah.

steps.m: of Allah (possessed by/connected to أَنصَارُ - helpers)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this connects to the word أَنصَارُ (helpers), meaning 'helpers of Allah'. Also added shadda on ل and changed initial alif to wasl form since this is how the word is written in the Quran.

steps.m: to trust or feel secure in something/someone
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of the root
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed the first letter to make it mean causing the action or entering into the state of the basic verb - here it means entering into a state of complete trust/security which is faith/belief
Added ت to show a female did the action

steps.m: and so she believed/had faith (referring to a group from بَنِىٓ إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened as a result of something mentioned before. In this ayah, it refers to a group from the Children of Israel who believed.

steps.m: to move in a circle or go around something
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of the root
Added vowels to make the root letters into a noun meaning 'the act of going around'
Changed the middle و to ا and added ِ to show who does the action
Changed the regular alif to a long alif (آ) for proper pronunciation
Added ة to make it refer to a group rather than an individual
Added shadda on ط because it comes after a word ending with a vowel

steps.m: a group (that moves together, subject of كَفَرَت)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is the subject doing the action of disbelieving (كَفَرَت) in this ayah
Basic preposition form meaning 'from' - this is a helper word that doesn't come from a root
Added shadda (double letter mark) to emphasize the م, which happens when certain letters come before it
Added a small ن mark (called nunation) because this word comes before a ب in بَنِىٓ. This is just a pronunciation helper.
This is the basic meaning of the root
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
These are actually two different words - not a transformation. بَنِي means 'children/descendants' while بَنَى means 'he built'. They just look similar but have different roots and meanings.
This is the same word as before - no actual change in this step

steps.m: Israel (proper name meaning 'one who struggles with God', part of بَنِى إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ - Children of Israel)
steps.n: This is a proper noun borrowed from Hebrew. It combines two parts: ישרא (yisra) meaning 'he struggles/strives' and אל (el) meaning 'God'. When used with بَنِى before it, it refers to the descendants of Prophet Jacob (يعقوب), who was given this name.
This is the basic meaning of the root
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb showing completed action. This word refers to a group (طَّآئِفَةٌ) mentioned in the ayah.

steps.m: she/it rejected/disbelieved (referring to طَّآئِفَةٌ - a group)
steps.n: Added feminine marker ت because the subject طَّآئِفَةٌ (a group) is feminine in Arabic

steps.m: and she/it rejected/disbelieved (contrasting with فَـَٔامَنَت - believed)
steps.n: Added و meaning 'and' to connect this with the previous phrase. This creates a contrast between the group that believed (فَـَٔامَنَت) and this group that disbelieved

steps.m: to move around or circle something

steps.n: Changed the middle letter و to ا and added ِ to show who does the action
Added ة to make it refer to a group rather than an individual
Added tanween dammah to make it an indefinite noun

steps.m: a group (that moves together) (subject of فَـَٔامَنَت)
steps.n: The ط gets a shadda (double letter) because it comes right after فَـ. The word means 'a group' and is doing the believing in this sentence.
Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to show intense strengthening. The fatha marks show this is past tense.
Added نا at the end to show 'we' (Allah) did the action. The sukun (ْ) is added before نا according to Arabic grammar rules.

steps.m: and so we strengthened intensely (those who believed)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened as a result of what came before. This word connects to 'those who believed' (ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟) in the ayah.
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun. It doesn't come from a root but is a built-in word in Arabic.
Adding ال makes it more specific, like saying 'the one' instead of just 'one'
Adding ين at the end makes it refer to multiple people instead of just one person
Adding the final vowel marks and changing ال to ٱ for proper pronunciation. This word is connected to ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding the long alif (ا) after the hamza makes the meaning stronger and more specific - changing from just feeling safe to actively believing in something

steps.m: they believed (referring to الَّذِينَ - those who)
steps.n: Adding وا shows that a group of people (all male or mixed) did this action. This word describes الَّذِينَ (those who) in the ayah.
This is a preposition that comes from the basic concept of being high or above something
Adding ى at the end makes it work as a preposition showing direction or opposition
The small alif (ٰ) is added to show that the ى is pronounced like a regular alif
Changes verb into noun meaning 'one who does the action'
Added shadda to intensify the meaning
Added kasra to prepare for possession suffix
Added هِمْ to show possession by them
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Adding 'أ' at the start changes the meaning from 'enter morning' to 'become' - like how morning brings change. This is a common pattern in Arabic where adding 'أ' creates new but related meanings.
The suffix وا indicates masculine plural past tense 'they'

steps.m: and so they became (victorious over عَدُوِّهِمْ their enemy)
steps.n: The فَ here shows this is the result of what came before - they were supported against their enemies, and so they became victorious
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Changed to active participle form showing who does the action
Added plural ending to show multiple people. This word comes after أَصْبَحُوا (they became) to show what state they ended up in.
